Explains the role of genetic testing to confirm the subtype/causative gene and guide prognosis and therapy; offers family counseling implications (inheritance/recurrence) at an L1-appropriate level.

Elicits key symptom pattern supporting congenital NMJ disease: fatigable weakness (often ocular/bulbar/proximal), onset in infancy/childhood (or lifelong symptoms), fluctuation with exertion, and absence of sensory symptoms.
Screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation: respiratory weakness (dyspnea, weak cough, hypoventilation), significant bulbar dysfunction (aspiration, choking), or rapid deterioration.
Avoids recommending immunosuppressive therapy or thymectomy as routine management for CMS, and avoids implying that AChR/MuSK antibodies are expected to be positive.

Communicates an initial treatment overview that includes pyridostigmine as a potential symptomatic option while noting that response varies by CMS subtype and should be guided by neuromuscular expertise.

Includes the safety counseling milestone to avoid macrolides due to potential worsening of neuromuscular transmission, and advises caution with other neuromuscular-impairing drugs (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium) without over-prescribing specifics.

If discussing any future prescribing, explicitly mentions that allergy, drug interaction, and contraindication checks must be performed before prescribing (even if no prescription is issued in this L1 task).
Uses clear, patient-friendly language, checks understanding, and invites questions; provides a plan for next steps (genetic testing and neuromuscular follow-up).

minimal_paths.description: Recognize CMS with glycosylation defect as the most likely diagnosis, explain it, and outline confirmatory genetic testing and safety counseling.
must_collect: fatigable weakness pattern (worse with exertion, better with rest)
age of onset (infancy/childhood or lifelong symptoms)
respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, weak cough, sleep-related hypoventilation)
family history/consanguinity or similarly affected relatives
medication exposures that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides)
absence of sensory loss/pain as primary feature
steps: Summarize key history features consistent with a congenital neuromuscular junction disorder and state the leading diagnosis: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, emphasizing it is genetic and differs from autoimmune myasthenia gravis.
Recommend confirmatory genetic testing and discuss implications for family counseling.
Provide initial management overview: symptomatic treatments may include pyridostigmine depending on subtype; counsel to avoid macrolides and other potential neuromuscular-worsening drugs.
Assess for urgent red flags (respiratory/bulbar compromise) and provide escalation instructions if present.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first with explicit differential and rationale before committing to CMS glycosylation defect, then documentation of final diagnosis.
steps: Elicit fatigability pattern, onset, ocular/bulbar/respiratory involvement, family history, and medication triggers.

Discuss differential briefly (autoimmune myasthenia gravis, mitochondrial myopathy, congenital myopathy, motor neuron disease depending on age) and explain why CMS is favored (early onset/lifelong, genetic context, fluctuating fatigability, antibody expectation).
Explain diagnosis and need for genetic testing; counsel on avoiding macrolides and mention pyridostigmine as potential therapy depending on subtype.
Document final diagnosis as congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect.

acceptable_variants.description: Prioritize safety/triage messaging when red flags are present while still meeting communication milestones and recording the diagnosis.
steps: Identify respiratory or severe bulbar symptoms and advise urgent evaluation/escalation (ED/urgent neuromuscular/respiratory assessment) while continuing the interview as feasible.
Explain suspected diagnosis (CMS with glycosylation defect) and that confirmation requires genetic testing.
Provide brief medication safety counseling (avoid macrolides; caution with other NMJ-impairing agents) and mention pyridostigmine as a possible symptomatic option to be guided by specialists.
Document diagnosis.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student/call center team lead. tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis to align treatment, counseling, and follow-up with iron deficiency anemia.
Initiates appropriate first-line therapy for iron deficiency anemia with oral iron.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "anemia" and "iron deficiency" in patient-friendly language.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes "oral iron" as first-line therapy.

Before prescribing, performs a contraindication check (critical) for oral iron (e.g., iron overload disorders such as hemochromatosis, repeated transfusions/known iron overload, or anemia not due to iron deficiency) and documents/addresses any red flags.

Before prescribing, performs a drug interaction check (critical) for oral iron, including separation from interacting agents that reduce absorption or are chelated (e.g., levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones, bisphosphonates, calcium/antacids/PPIs), and provides spacing instructions when relevant.
Before prescribing, asks about allergies (non-critical) and avoids prescribing if a relevant allergy/intolerance is identified; offers an alternative formulation or plan if needed.
Provides clear dosing and administration guidance for oral iron (how to take, spacing from interacting meds/foods, and what to do if GI side effects occur).

Counsels on expected timeline of response and common adverse effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and gives safety-net advice for concerning symptoms (e.g., melena, hematochezia, syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea).

Addresses likely etiologies and the need to evaluate the cause of iron deficiency (dietary deficiency, menstrual blood loss, GI blood loss) and does not present iron therapy as sufficient without considering source of blood loss when appropriate.
Communicates uncertainty appropriately if the cause is not yet established and proposes follow-up to confirm response and reassess diagnosis if no improvement.

history suggesting contraindication to iron (hemochromatosis/iron overload, repeated transfusions, known non–iron-deficiency anemia)
steps: Confirm clinical picture consistent with anemia and iron deficiency; ensure no unstable symptoms requiring urgent care.

Ask about allergies/intolerances relevant to iron preparations.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the agent must correctly identify and record Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and nonpharmacologic management planning.

nl_assertions: Elicits key neurologic history suggestive of hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy (slowly progressive distal weakness, foot drop, frequent ankle sprains/tripping, pes cavus/hammer toes, distal numbness/paresthesias, onset in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood, family history).

Performs or describes a focused neurologic exam including distal muscle atrophy/weakness, reduced/absent ankle reflexes, distal sensory loss, and gait assessment; notes foot deformities (pes cavus) if present.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and frames it as a usually slowly progressive inherited peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves.

Discusses the role of genetic testing explicitly (uses the phrase "genetic testing") for confirmation, subtype identification, prognosis, and family planning; clarifies that testing may not identify a variant in all cases.

Explains treatment emphasizing functional management and safety: includes "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as core interventions; may also mention ankle-foot orthoses, stretching/strengthening, balance training, and fall prevention.

Mentions appropriate diagnostic evaluation options without overpromising (e.g., nerve conduction studies/EMG to characterize neuropathy; genetic testing; considers referral to neuromuscular neurology/genetics as appropriate), while keeping the primary task as diagnosis.

Addresses red flags and alternative diagnoses briefly (e.g., rapid progression, asymmetric deficits, prominent pain, systemic symptoms) and indicates these would prompt evaluation for acquired neuropathies.
Avoids inappropriate or unsafe claims (e.g., does not state there is a curative medication; does not recommend neurotoxic medications as treatment).

If any prescribing is proposed, performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies before prescribing (critical), and considers drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical).
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides realistic expectations about progression and functional support.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize a classic presentation of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease based on progressive distal weakness/foot deformities and family history; provide diagnosis explanation and confirmatory testing plan.
must_collect: progressive distal leg/foot weakness or foot drop
frequent tripping/ankle sprains or gait difficulty
foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) or history of high arches
family history of similar symptoms or known neuropathy
age of onset and tempo (slow progression)
steps: Take focused history for hereditary peripheral neuropathy (motor and sensory symptoms, onset, progression, family history, functional impact).
Perform focused neuro/MSK exam (distal strength, reflexes—especially ankles, sensation, gait, foot structure).
State working diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; explain what it is and expected course in plain language.
Discuss confirmation/workup: genetic testing and consideration of EMG/NCS; offer counseling regarding implications for relatives.
Outline first-line management: physical therapy and orthotics; include fall prevention and supportive care advice.
Document the diagnosis via the required tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential (e.g., chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, diabetic neuropathy, lumbar radiculopathy) before concluding CMT is most likely given slow progression, distal pattern, deformities, and family history; still completes required diagnosis recording.
steps: Elicit history and exam features of distal symmetric neuropathy and foot deformities; assess tempo and family history.
Briefly consider and rule-in/rule-out common alternatives based on red flags (rapid progression, pain, systemic illness, proximal weakness).
Explain Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and the role of genetic testing; discuss EMG/NCS as supportive testing.
Recommend physical therapy and orthotics as mainstays; provide safety/fall-prevention guidance.
Record diagnosis with the required tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds referral suggestions in narrative (neuromuscular neurology, genetics, orthotics/prosthetics) without using referral WRITE tools (since L1 requires only diagnosis tool), while still meeting communication milestones.
steps: Gather key symptoms (distal weakness/foot drop, sensory symptoms, deformities) and family history; perform focused exam.
Communicate diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; explain inheritance and variability; introduce genetic testing for confirmation/subtyping.
Discuss supportive care: physical therapy, orthotics, and monitoring for complications; mention potential referrals as part of care planning.
Record diagnosis with the required tool.
